claims.claim_text: The October 2021 social-media outage anecdote includes precise duration, cause, internal-access details, and estimated revenue loss.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, first paragraph
claims.anchor_text: In October 2021, a major social media company experienced a worldwide outage lasting over six hours. Billions of users could not access any of the company's services. The root cause was a routine BGP configuration change that accidentally withdrew the routes advertising the company's DNS nameservers. Because their DNS was unreachable, every subsequent DNS lookup for their domain failed. Even their internal tools for diagnosing and fixing the issue relied on the same DNS infrastructure, creating a devastating feedback loop. Engineers could not access internal dashboards, could not SSH into servers, and even physical access to data centers was impacted because the badge entry systems depended on the same network. The estimated revenue loss exceeded $100 million.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A control-plane or routing failure can make authoritative DNS unreachable, which can take dependent services and even recovery tooling offline. The operational lesson is to design DNS and break-glass access paths so they do not depend on the same failing control plane.
claims.rationale: This paragraph bundles multiple precise incident claims, some of which are off-allowlist or not directly supported by an allowlisted primary source, so the safest repair is to keep the lesson and remove the false precision.

claims.claim_text: Creating a public Cloud DNS zone assigns four authoritative nameservers from the `googledomains.com` pool.
claims.span_hint: section: Public Zones: Internet-Facing DNS, first paragraph
claims.rationale: Google Cloud documentation shows Cloud DNS returning four `ns-cloud-*.googledomains.com` nameservers for a managed public zone.

claims.claim_text: Before a migration, lower TTL to 60 seconds at least 24 hours in advance so caches pick up the new IP quickly.
claims.anchor_text: **Pro tip**: Before a planned migration, lower the TTL to 60 seconds at least 24 hours in advance (equal to the current TTL). This ensures that by migration time, all caches have the short TTL and will pick up the new IP quickly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Pro tip**: Before a planned migration, lower the TTL at least one full current-TTL interval in advance so existing cached answers can expire before the cutover.
claims.rationale: Google documents the TTL-expiry principle, but this exact numeric recipe is derived from the example rather than stated verbatim in an allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: Cloud DNS supports routing policies based on weight, geolocation, and failover/health checks.
claims.span_hint: section: DNS Routing Policies, intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Google's routing-policies overview explicitly documents weighted round robin, geolocation routing, and health-check-based failover behavior.

claims.claim_text: Private DNS zones are visible only from authorized VPC networks.
claims.span_hint: section: Private Zones: Internal DNS, first paragraph
claims.rationale: Google's Cloud DNS terminology defines private zones as queryable only by the VPC networks you authorize.

claims.claim_text: The GKE-integration paragraph overstates automatic inheritance and default forwarding behavior for private-zone resolution.
claims.anchor_text: When you create a private DNS zone in a VPC, Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) clusters in that VPC automatically inherit this resolution capability. By default, the cluster's DNS provider (like `kube-dns` or Cloud DNS for GKE) forwards queries for non-cluster domains to the VPC network's metadata server. This allows your Kubernetes pods to seamlessly resolve and connect to legacy VMs, Cloud SQL instances, and other GCP services using your private Cloud DNS records.
claims.suggested_rewrite: GKE nodes participate in Google Cloud DNS resolution for their network, and Cloud DNS for GKE integrates cluster DNS records with Google Cloud's DNS path. In practice, cluster workloads can resolve VPC-visible names when the cluster's DNS mode and scope are configured to expose them.
claims.rationale: Google documents several GKE DNS modes and scopes, so the blanket 'automatic inheritance' and default-provider behavior in the original text is too broad.

claims.claim_text: The simplified private-zone resolution-order block is incomplete versus Google's documented order.
claims.span_hint: section: Private Zone Resolution Order, intro line
claims.anchor_text: When a VM in a VPC makes a DNS query, Cloud DNS resolves it in this order:
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a VM or GKE node uses Google Cloud DNS, the official resolution order includes alternative name servers and response policies before private, forwarding, peering, internal, and public DNS lookups; use the Google Cloud documentation for the exact sequence:
claims.rationale: The official Google Cloud resolution order includes additional steps that the module's simplified list omits, so a citation alone would not honestly support it.

claims.claim_text: Cloud DNS forwarding lets you send queries for specific domains to external DNS servers in hybrid environments.
claims.span_hint: section: DNS Forwarding: Hybrid Cloud DNS, intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Google documents forwarding zones as a private-zone type that targets on-premises or other DNS servers for outbound forwarding.

claims.claim_text: Inbound DNS forwarding uses a server policy that creates VPC entry-point IPs for on-premises resolvers.
claims.rationale: Google's server-policies documentation describes inbound server policy entry points sourced from subnet ranges to expose VPC name resolution to on-premises networks.

claims.claim_text: DNS peering lets one VPC resolve names via another VPC's DNS without requiring VPC Network Peering.
claims.span_hint: section: DNS Peering: Cross-VPC Resolution, first paragraph
claims.rationale: Google explicitly distinguishes DNS peering from VPC Network Peering and states that VPC Network Peering is not required for DNS peering.

claims.claim_text: DNSSEC protects against DNS spoofing/poisoning, and Cloud DNS supports DNSSEC for public zones.
claims.span_hint: section: DNSSEC: Securing DNS, intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Google's DNSSEC overview explains the security property and documents DNSSEC support for Cloud DNS managed public zones.

claims.claim_text: The '100% availability SLA / highest SLA / hundreds of PoPs / region-down continuity' item mixes supported facts with unsupported superlatives and resilience specifics.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Cloud DNS guarantees 100% availability SLA**---one of the highest SLAs in all of GCP. It achieves this by using Google's global Anycast network, which means DNS queries are served from the nearest of hundreds of Google Points of Presence worldwide. Even if an entire region goes down, DNS continues to resolve from other locations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Cloud DNS publishes a 100% serving-DNS-queries SLO in its SLA** and uses anycast name servers to serve zones from multiple locations around the world for high availability and low latency.
claims.rationale: The SLA and anycast facts are supportable, but the superlative comparison, PoP count, and explicit region-failure claim are not directly supported together by a single allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The claim that Cloud DNS has a maximum TTL of 86,400 seconds and that propagation can take 48 hours is not directly supported.
claims.anchor_text: 2. **The maximum TTL you can set in Cloud DNS is 86,400 seconds (24 hours)**, but many recursive resolvers cache records for longer if the SOA record's minimum TTL suggests it. In practice, when you change a DNS record, expect full propagation to take up to 48 hours for some edge cases, even if your TTL is set to 300 seconds, because some resolvers do not respect TTLs strictly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **Resolvers can continue serving cached answers until the relevant TTLs expire**, and some resolvers or client-side caches might not refresh exactly when you expect. For planned changes, lower TTLs ahead of time and verify propagation against authoritative name servers.
claims.rationale: Google documents TTL-driven caching and warns that some resolvers use their own values, but this exact maximum-TTL and 48-hour propagation framing is not directly backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: A private zone for the same domain overrides the public zone for VMs in that VPC, and missing records return NXDOMAIN rather than falling back to public DNS.
claims.rationale: Google's overlapping-zones documentation gives this exact private-over-public behavior and NXDOMAIN outcome for missing records in the private zone.

claims.claim_text: Cloud DNS does not literally support RPZ zones as stated here; Google documents response policies, which are similar to but different from RPZs.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Cloud DNS supports Response Policy Zones (RPZs)**, which let you override DNS responses for specific domains. This is effectively a DNS-level firewall---you can block known malicious domains by returning `NXDOMAIN` or redirect them to a sinkhole IP. Enterprises use RPZs to enforce security policies without deploying additional infrastructure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Cloud DNS supports response policies**, which let you override answers for selected names inside your network by serving local DNS data or using passthrough exceptions.
claims.rationale: Google explicitly says response policies achieve results similar to RPZs but are not DNS zones, so the original wording overstates the product capability.

further_reading.title: Cloud DNS overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the core service model, public versus private zones, anycast serving, and propagation behavior.

further_reading.title: Name resolution order
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents the exact lookup sequence for VMs and GKE nodes, including policies, private zones, peering, and public DNS.

further_reading.title: Use Cloud DNS for GKE
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains Cloud DNS integration modes for GKE, including cluster scope, VPC scope, and how GKE DNS resolution works.

notes: No shared citation pool was assumed, so `source_ids` are empty and direct candidate URLs are carried in `proposed_url`. The multi-line 'Private Zone Resolution Order' block is materially incomplete versus the official Google order and likely needs manual editorial replacement, not just a citation.
